headlineSummary: HN sentiment is mostly neutral overall: Anthropic draws mixed but slightly more favorable coding feedback, while Microsoft Copilot and OpenAI sit in a close tie with limited evidence and mixed-neutral reactions [E1] [E2] [E3].

ranking.summary: Anthropic gets mixed-neutral feedback with one strong critique of Claude Code’s edge-case reliability and one positive note that Claude is better at writing intuitive, maintainable code [E1] [E2].

Microsoft Copilot is described neutrally but in a close tie with OpenAI, with limited evidence from a comment that praises the older integrated Copilot experience and questions the chatbot-style replacement [E3].
OpenAI sits in a close tie with Microsoft Copilot on limited evidence, with Codex described as strong at finding corner cases and doing reviews but also as overengineered and pedantic [E2].

evidence.excerpt: Claude Code is extremely eager to reach the end goal to the point that it feels like a fever dream to write code with it. In the end, I have low confidence about edge cases and fit into the project's architectural and design goals.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names Claude Code and strongly criticizes its coding behavior and the user's confidence in its output.

evidence.excerpt: Then implement with Claude 4.7 Max - which runs slower, but does a better job. Then review the implementation, then have Codex GPT 5.5 xhigh fast review it - which almost always finds corner cases. Have Claude fix those - Claude is better at writing intuitive maintainable code versus Codex overengineered/shortcut filled code. (Codex is better at finding/fixing bugs and doing reviews - it's annoyingly pedantic)

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly names Claude and says it 'does a better job' and is 'better at writing intuitive maintainable code,' despite noting it is slower.

The excerpt explicitly names Codex GPT and gives mixed evaluation: it 'almost always finds corner cases' and is 'better at finding/fixing bugs and doing reviews,' but its code is described as 'overengineered/shortcut filled' and 'annoyingly pedantic'.

evidence.excerpt: Remember when Copilot was basically a super-smart version of Intellisense? It was awesome. Sure, there was a lot of pushback and concern, mainly about licensing and ethical issues, none of which are solved with the current chatbot model. But now I also have to come up with a prompt and type it out. How is that an improvement over having the LLM use surrounding code as context and figure out how to fill in the blanks?

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names Copilot, praises its earlier integrated coding-assistant form as 'awesome,' but criticizes the current chatbot-style experience as a downgrade.
